overview.whyItMatters: Calling foundation models correctly is fundamental. Understanding synchronous vs asynchronous patterns, streaming responses, error handling, and intelligent routing determines whether your application is reliable and performant or frustrating and flaky.

concepts.title: InvokeModel vs Converse: Choosing the Right API
keyPoints: InvokeModel: Model-native format, full parameter access
Converse: Unified format, model-agnostic code
Converse simplifies tool calling with standardized schema
Use Converse for multi-model apps; InvokeModel for model-specific features
concepts.examTip: 'Model-agnostic' or 'switch models easily' or 'unified API' → Converse API. 'Model-specific parameters' → InvokeModel.

concepts.title: Synchronous and Asynchronous FM Calls
concepts.content:
Foundation model APIs can operate in two fundamentally different modes: synchronous, where the caller waits for the complete response, and asynchronous, where the request is submitted for later processing. Understanding when to use each pattern is essential for building applications that perform well under real-world conditions.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph SYNC["Synchronous"]
    S1[Client] --> S2[Request]
    S2 --> S3["WAIT..."]
    S3 --> S4[Response]
    end
    subgraph ASYNC["Asynchronous"]
    A1[Client] --> A2[Submit]
    A2 --> A3[job_id]
    A3 --> A4[Poll/Callback]
    A4 --> A5[Response]
    end
    subgraph STREAM["Streaming"]
    T1[Client] --> T2[Request]
    T2 --> T3[Token 1]
    T3 --> T4[Token 2]
    T4 --> T5[Token N]
    T5 --> T6[DONE]
    end
```

Synchronous calls using Bedrock's InvokeModel API represent the simplest integration pattern. Your application sends a request, the connection stays open while the model processes, and you receive the complete response when generation finishes. This pattern works well for interactive applications where users expect immediate responses and are willing to wait a few seconds. Any AWS compute service can make synchronous Bedrock calls—Lambda, EC2, ECS, or even client applications with proper credentials.

The challenge with synchronous calls is that foundation model inference takes time. Complex prompts with long outputs can require 10-30 seconds of processing. API Gateway imposes a 29-second timeout, which can be exceeded by complex requests. Users staring at loading spinners may abandon the application. For these scenarios, you need alternatives.

Asynchronous patterns decouple request submission from response delivery. While Bedrock doesn't provide a native async API, you can build async patterns around it using AWS services. Amazon SQS serves as a buffer: producers submit requests to a queue, Lambda functions consume from the queue and call Bedrock, and results flow to an output queue, SNS notification, or storage location. This architecture handles traffic spikes gracefully because the queue absorbs bursts that would overwhelm synchronous processing.

Batch inference takes asynchronous processing further by handling many requests together. SageMaker batch transform jobs accept files of inputs from S3, process them efficiently, and write results back to S3. This approach optimizes throughput for bulk processing scenarios like document analysis, data enrichment, or offline content generation where real-time response isn't required.
keyPoints: InvokeModel: Synchronous, caller waits for response
SQS + Lambda: Build async patterns for background processing
Batch inference: Process many requests together for efficiency
Match pattern to latency tolerance and use case

concepts.title: Streaming FM Responses
keyPoints: InvokeModelWithResponseStream for streaming responses
SSE is simplest for server-to-client streaming
Streaming improves perceived latency significantly
Buffer when you need to validate/filter before display

concepts.title: Building Resilient FM Integrations
concepts.content:
Foundation model APIs, like any distributed system, can experience failures. Models might be temporarily unavailable, requests might timeout, or rate limits might be exceeded. Production applications must handle these situations gracefully rather than crashing or confusing users.

Exponential backoff is the foundation of resilient API integration. When a request fails with a retriable error, you wait before trying again—but you wait longer after each successive failure. First retry after 100ms, then 200ms, then 400ms, and so on. This prevents overwhelming a recovering service with retry storms and avoids the thundering herd problem where many clients retry simultaneously after an outage. The AWS SDK implements exponential backoff automatically for retriable errors; you just need to ensure it's not disabled.

Rate limiting deserves special attention because foundation model services have usage quotas. Bedrock and SageMaker impose account-level limits on requests per second and tokens per minute. API Gateway can enforce application-level limits to prevent any single user or client from consuming all available capacity. Implement usage plans and API keys for per-client throttling, returning HTTP 429 (Too Many Requests) with a Retry-After header rather than failing unpredictably.

Circuit breakers prevent wasted effort when a service is consistently failing. The pattern works like an electrical circuit breaker: after a threshold number of failures, the circuit "opens" and requests fail immediately without attempting the call. After a cooldown period, the circuit "half-opens" to test if the service has recovered. If the test succeeds, normal operation resumes; if it fails, the cooldown continues. This protects both your application (from wasting resources on failing calls) and the downstream service (from being overwhelmed during recovery).

Fallback strategies provide alternatives when the primary path fails. You might return cached responses for queries similar to ones you've handled before. You might fall back to a different model—if Claude Sonnet is unavailable, perhaps Claude Haiku can provide a degraded but acceptable response. You might implement graceful degradation that returns partial results or honest messages about limited functionality. For non-urgent requests, you might accept the request and queue it for processing when the service recovers.

AWS X-Ray provides the observability needed to understand failures and performance issues. Distributed tracing tracks requests as they flow through your system—API Gateway to Lambda to Bedrock and back. You can see exactly where time is spent, identify bottlenecks, and debug failures by examining the complete request trace rather than scattered logs.
keyPoints: Exponential backoff: Built into AWS SDK for retriable errors
API Gateway rate limiting: Prevent capacity exhaustion
Circuit breakers: Stop calling failing services
Fallback: Cache, alternative models, graceful degradation
concepts.examTip: 'Resilient' or 'handle failures gracefully' -> exponential backoff, circuit breakers, fallback strategies.

concepts.title: Intelligent Request Routing
concepts.content:
Not every request should go to the same model or endpoint. Different queries have different requirements—some need sophisticated reasoning, others just need fast responses, some contain sensitive data requiring special handling. Intelligent routing directs requests to appropriate destinations based on content, cost constraints, performance requirements, or system state.

Static routing uses predefined rules that don't change based on request content. API Gateway can route based on URL path: requests to /simple might go to Claude Haiku while /complex goes to Claude Sonnet. This approach is simple to implement and understand, but it puts the burden of classification on the caller. If clients can't reliably classify their own requests, static routing produces suboptimal results.

Content-based dynamic routing analyzes request content to make routing decisions at runtime. A fast, cheap classifier (potentially a small model or rule-based system) examines the incoming query and determines its complexity, topic, or requirements. Based on this classification, the request routes to the appropriate model. Step Functions provides clean orchestration for this pattern: a classification state feeds into a choice state that routes to different model invocation states.

Metric-based routing considers current system state rather than request content. If the primary endpoint shows high latency or elevated error rates, traffic shifts to a secondary endpoint. CloudWatch metrics drive this routing through alarms that trigger Lambda functions or update routing configuration. This provides automatic failover and load balancing based on real-time health.

API Gateway's request transformation capabilities add another dimension to routing. Before requests reach their destination, you can modify them: adding system prompts, injecting user context, formatting for specific model requirements, or applying guardrails. This centralizes transformation logic in the gateway rather than scattering it across application code.

Common routing use cases include cost optimization (sending simple queries to cheaper models), performance optimization (routing latency-sensitive requests to provisioned throughput endpoints), compliance requirements (directing requests with sensitive data to specific regions or endpoints), and A/B testing (sending a percentage of traffic to experimental models or prompts for comparison).
keyPoints: Static routing: Path-based, simple rules
Content-based: Classify then route to appropriate model
Metric-based: Route based on system health/load
Step Functions orchestrates complex routing logic

skillExplanations.explanation:
Synchronous foundation model calls can originate from any AWS compute environment. Lambda functions call Bedrock's InvokeModel API directly using the AWS SDK. EC2 instances and ECS containers do the same. Even client applications can make direct calls if they have appropriate AWS credentials, though this is less common in production due to credential management concerns.

The AWS SDK handles the mechanics of authentication, request serialization, and response parsing. Use the latest SDK version to benefit from recent improvements and bug fixes. Configure retry behavior appropriately—the defaults work for many cases, but you might need longer timeouts for complex prompts that require more processing time. Model inference can take 10-30 seconds for sophisticated reasoning tasks, which exceeds default timeout settings in many frameworks.

API Gateway adds a managed layer between clients and your foundation model integration. It handles authentication through IAM, Cognito, or API keys. It enforces throttling to prevent capacity exhaustion. It can cache responses for repeated queries. It provides monitoring and logging. For production APIs, fronting Bedrock calls with API Gateway adds important controls and observability.

awsServices.whenToUse: InvokeModel API for synchronous FM calls from any compute.
Front-end for Bedrock APIs. Validation, throttling, authentication.

skillExplanations.explanation:
Asynchronous processing patterns become necessary when you need to handle traffic that exceeds your synchronous processing capacity, when requests take too long for interactive response, or when you want to decouple request submission from processing for reliability.

Amazon SQS provides the foundation for async patterns. Clients submit requests to a queue and immediately receive confirmation—no waiting for model inference. Lambda functions configured as SQS consumers pull messages and process them, calling Bedrock's InvokeModel for each. Results flow to an output queue, trigger SNS notifications, or write to DynamoDB or S3 for later retrieval.

This architecture handles traffic spikes naturally because SQS absorbs bursts that would overwhelm direct API calls. It provides reliability because messages persist until successfully processed. It enables retry logic because failed messages return to the queue for reprocessing.

SageMaker batch transform extends this to bulk processing scenarios. You prepare input data in S3, configure a batch transform job specifying the model and instance type, and receive output in S3 when processing completes. This optimizes for throughput rather than latency, making it ideal for processing large datasets offline.

awsServices.whenToUse: Queue-based async processing. Decouple submission from inference.
Process queue messages with InvokeModel in Lambda consumers.

skillExplanations.explanation:
Streaming APIs fundamentally change the user experience by delivering tokens incrementally. InvokeModelWithResponseStream opens a connection that delivers chunks as the model generates them. Each chunk contains partial output—typically a few tokens—that your application processes immediately.

Processing streaming responses requires iterating through the event stream and handling different event types. Content deltas contain actual generated text. Message stop events indicate completion. Error events signal problems. Your code must handle each appropriately, accumulating content while displaying it to users.

Server-Sent Events represents the standard transport for delivering streams to web clients. The client opens a single HTTP connection using the EventSource API. The server sends events as they become available. SSE works through proxies and load balancers because it uses standard HTTP. It's simpler than WebSockets because it's unidirectional—server to client only—which matches the foundation model use case where the server streams generated text to the client.

WebSockets provide bidirectional streaming and might be appropriate when you need to send new input while streaming is in progress—for example, letting users interrupt or redirect generation mid-stream. For most foundation model applications, SSE provides the necessary functionality with less complexity.

awsServices.whenToUse: Proxy streaming responses to clients. WebSocket APIs for bidirectional.

skillExplanations.explanation:
Building resilient integrations requires multiple complementary strategies. The AWS SDK provides exponential backoff automatically for retriable errors—you don't need to implement it yourself, but you should understand that it's happening and configure appropriate retry counts and timeouts.

API Gateway rate limiting prevents capacity exhaustion at the application level. Set usage plans that allocate capacity across clients. Return HTTP 429 responses with Retry-After headers when limits are exceeded. This provides clients with clear feedback about throttling rather than mysterious failures.

Circuit breakers require explicit implementation in your code or through Step Functions. Track failure rates over recent requests. When failures exceed a threshold, stop making calls for a cooldown period. After cooldown, allow a test request through. If it succeeds, resume normal operation; if it fails, extend the cooldown. This prevents wasting resources on known-failing services and gives failing services time to recover.

AWS X-Ray provides the observability foundation for understanding failures and performance. Instrument your Lambda functions and SDK calls. Trace maps visualize request flow and identify slow components. When something fails, trace data shows exactly what happened and where. For complex GenAI pipelines with multiple service calls, X-Ray is essential for debugging production issues.

awsServices.whenToUse: Distributed tracing for debugging and performance analysis.
SDK with retry configuration for resilient FM calls.

skillExplanations.explanation:
Intelligent routing optimizes cost, performance, and reliability by directing requests to appropriate destinations. The simplest form uses API Gateway path-based routing or Lambda function logic with static rules. This requires manual configuration but provides predictable behavior.

Content-based dynamic routing examines request characteristics to make routing decisions. A classification step—potentially using a small, fast model—determines query complexity, topic, or requirements. Step Functions orchestrates this cleanly: a classification Lambda feeds results to a choice state that branches to different model invocations. Simple queries route to fast, cheap models. Complex queries route to more capable models. Sensitive queries route to specialized endpoints.

Metric-based routing responds to system state rather than request content. CloudWatch metrics track endpoint latency, error rates, and queue depths. When primary endpoints show degraded performance, routing shifts traffic to alternatives. This can be implemented through Step Functions checking metrics, Lambda functions updating routing configuration, or API Gateway integration with CloudWatch.

API Gateway request transformations modify requests before routing. You might add system prompts that vary by route, inject user context from authentication, or format requests for specific model requirements. This centralizes transformation logic rather than implementing it in every client.

awsServices.whenToUse: Orchestrate content-based and metric-based routing decisions.
Multiple models as routing targets for different query types.

values.Server-Sent Events (SSE): Streaming FM responses to web clients
values.WebSockets: Interactive chat with user input during streaming
values.Polling: Legacy clients that can't use SSE/WS

examStrategies.approach: Converse API provides unified interface across all models. Same code works for Claude, Titan, Llama, Mistral. Change only the modelId parameter to switch models.
InvokeModelWithResponseStream + SSE or WebSocket through API Gateway. SSE is simpler for server-to-client streaming. WebSocket only if bidirectional is needed.
Exponential backoff (SDK default), circuit breakers, fallback to alternative models or cached responses. X-Ray for debugging failures.
Step Functions for content-based routing. API Gateway for path-based static routing. CloudWatch metrics for health-based routing.

keyTakeaways: Converse API for model-agnostic code; InvokeModel for model-specific features
InvokeModel for sync, SQS + Lambda for async, InvokeModelWithResponseStream for streaming
SSE is the standard for streaming FM responses to web clients
Exponential backoff + circuit breakers + fallbacks = resilient integration
X-Ray traces requests across services for debugging
Step Functions orchestrates intelligent content-based routing
commonMistakes: Not using streaming for user-facing applications (poor UX)
Missing timeout configuration (default may be too short for FM)
No fallback strategy when FM calls fail
Polling instead of SSE/WebSocket for real-time updates
Hardcoding model selection instead of intelligent routing
